acceptance_targets: [CH06_CONTINUITY_DEPUTIES_ARRIVAL] At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:21, the revised sentence no longer claims a 7:00 deputy arrival. A reader comparing the line to the continuity sheet should be able to reconcile the order as call around 7 AM, deputies after that, evidence already removed by the time Nate checks the room.

[CH06_VOICEMAIL_TOO_COMPOSED] In the revised passage around snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:31 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:33, the voicemail still clearly contains death-language that could alarm a detective, but it must also show at least one audible restart, self-interruption, or doubling-back and at least one low, character-true insult or phrasing choice that feels messier than podcast copy. If the passage can still be read straight through as a polished miniature stand-up routine, it fails.

[CH06_PATHOLOGY_REEXPLAINED] Reading the revised sequences around snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:37-39, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:117-121, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:181-193, each surviving interior gloss must add a new fact, new cost, or new leverage shift beyond 'Nate narrates everything.' If any later paragraph can be removed without losing plot movement or character-state change because it only restates that thesis, the revision fails.

[CH06_ENDING_OVEREXPLAINS] After the revised beats around snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:199-207, the chapter must finish on an external consequence or sensory image tied to Mallick's note-taking and Nate's fresh exposure. The closing may include brief interiority, but it may not restate the chapter's meaning in abstract thesis language or imagined episode-title form once the confession-notebook beat has already landed.

[MOTIF_PUNCT_CH06] Pass if chapter 06 contains at most one refrigerator reference and uses Nate-specific ambient detail for the remaining scene punctuation. Fail if two or more refrigerator mentions remain.

findings.acceptance_test: At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:21, the revised sentence no longer claims a 7:00 deputy arrival. A reader comparing the line to the continuity sheet should be able to reconcile the order as call around 7 AM, deputies after that, evidence already removed by the time Nate checks the room.
findings.problem: The chapter states that the county deputies 'arrived at seven,' but the continuity sheet fixes about 7:00 AM as the time the group calls police and about 8:30 AM as the deputy arrival. That creates a direct timeline contradiction in a housekeeping beat the novel uses to track when evidence leaves the room.
findings.rewrite_direction: Revise the timing language at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:21 so Nate's recollection matches the canon sequence: the call goes out around 7 AM, deputies arrive later, and Mallick comes later still. If Nate is being approximate, make the approximation explicit without asserting a false arrival time; preserve the point that Martin's phone is already bagged and unavailable.

findings.acceptance_test: In the revised passage around snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:31 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:33, the voicemail still clearly contains death-language that could alarm a detective, but it must also show at least one audible restart, self-interruption, or doubling-back and at least one low, character-true insult or phrasing choice that feels messier than podcast copy. If the passage can still be read straight through as a polished miniature stand-up routine, it fails.
findings.problem: The quoted voicemail, which is the chapter's core dialogue object, reads like a finished set piece instead of Nate's messier, drinkier, more profane public register. The balanced funeral imagery, clean one-liners, and radio-ready escalation make the bit feel authored rather than spoken, which weakens both Nate's style-bible voice and the plausibility that this is how the group normally razzes one another.
findings.rewrite_direction: Roughen the voicemail material at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:31 and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:33, then tune Nate's later paraphrase at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:175 to match. Keep the incriminating death language, but let the voicemail break, repeat, self-correct, and get a little uglier or stupider in a way consistent with Nate's style-bible profile rather than landing as a polished monologue.

findings.acceptance_test: Reading the revised sequences around snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:37-39, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:117-121, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:181-193, each surviving interior gloss must add a new fact, new cost, or new leverage shift beyond 'Nate narrates everything.' If any later paragraph can be removed without losing plot movement or character-state change because it only restates that thesis, the revision fails.
findings.problem: The chapter keeps stopping to explain, in abstract, that Nate narrates everything and turns life into content. Once the corpse-and-phone memory and the Mallick interview have dramatized that pathology, these repeated diagnostic paragraphs start restating the same insight instead of escalating the comic-paranoia engine.
findings.rewrite_direction: Compress or repurpose the explanatory glosses at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:39, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:117, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:181, and snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:193. Keep one early articulation of Nate's podcast-brain as setup, then make later interior beats earn their space by changing pressure: his talk should materially worsen the interview, expose a new fear, or alter how Mallick reads him instead of re-diagnosing the same trait.

findings.acceptance_test: After the revised beats around snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:199-207, the chapter must finish on an external consequence or sensory image tied to Mallick's note-taking and Nate's fresh exposure. The closing may include brief interiority, but it may not restate the chapter's meaning in abstract thesis language or imagined episode-title form once the confession-notebook beat has already landed.
findings.problem: The scene already lands when Mallick restates the tampering cleanly and then thanks Nate. The long italicized thought afterward re-translates the beat into chapter-theme language and imagined podcast packaging, which blunts the cleaner dread the interview has already earned.
findings.rewrite_direction: Rewrite the closing movement at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:205, using snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_06.md:207 as the preferred landing zone. End on a concrete aftermath image or consequence already present in the scene, such as the notebook, the unanswered Mia text, the pen scratch, the draft board, or the lake going dark, rather than another imagined segment outline. Keep Nate's self-awareness only if it serves the fresh external consequence.

findings.acceptance_test: Pass if chapter 06 contains at most one refrigerator reference and uses Nate-specific ambient detail for the remaining scene punctuation. Fail if two or more refrigerator mentions remain.
findings.evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:1562 refrigerator reference; snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:1596 'A loon called from across the water, the sound arriving across the flat surface'; snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:1690 refrigerator; snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:1724 'the house was quiet except for the refrigerator'

evidence.excerpt: 1562: The ceiling above him creaked. Someone walking through the kitchen. The old refrigerator shuddered into its loud cycle — a compressor grinding to life with the shuddering complaint it made every forty minutes, a rhythm Nate had been unconsciously tracking for three days because that was what his brain did, it cataloged patterns and produced commentary and converted raw experience into material. Even now. Even here. He was filing the refrigerator's cycle under "ambient texture" and Martin's death under "season-ending injury" and his own terror under "character development," and he could not make himself stop.

1596: Nate put the phone in his lap and looked at the lake. A loon called from across the water, the sound arriving across the flat surface with the clarity of something designed to be heard at distance. He thought about how the voicemail would sound played back in a courtroom. He thought about how it would sound on a true crime podcast — not his podcast, a real one, one with listeners, one that would play the recording with the key phrases captioned on screen:

1690: The kitchen was quiet. The refrigerator was between cycles. Through the window above the sink, Nate could see the last light on the lake, flat and amber and indifferent to what was happening at this table. He looked at Mallick's notebook, open to a page covered in her handwriting, small and even, and he thought about the eleven other guys at Hennepin County Public Defender who'd sat across a table from a client and said *don't talk to the police, just don't talk*, and he thought about how none of those clients had been fantasy football podcasters whose professional skill set was talking and whose personal pathology was talking and whose response to every stimulus the world had ever offered them was to open their mouth and narrate it until the stimulus became content.

1724: His phone sat in his pocket. He still hadn't called Mia back. Through the kitchen window the lake was going dark, the amber light pulling off the surface in a long retreat, and the loons were starting up across the water, and the house was quiet except for the refrigerator and the settling of old wood and the scratch of a pen from wherever Mallick had gone, making notes about the man who'd talked himself into exactly the place he'd spent all day trying to talk himself out of.

rewrite_direction.excerpt: 1562: The ceiling above him creaked. Someone walking through the kitchen. The old refrigerator shuddered into its loud cycle — a compressor grinding to life with the shuddering complaint it made every forty minutes, a rhythm Nate had been unconsciously tracking for three days because that was what his brain did, it cataloged patterns and produced commentary and converted raw experience into material. Even now. Even here. He was filing the refrigerator's cycle under "ambient texture" and Martin's death under "season-ending injury" and his own terror under "character development," and he could not make himself stop.

findings.problem: Three refrigerator references and one loon call in Nate's chapter. Nate's anxious interiority should be punctuated by his own nervous habits, not by the house's default ambient package.
findings.rewrite_direction: Revise snapshots/cycle_01/FINAL_NOVEL.md:1562, 1690, 1724. Cut two of the three refrigerator references. Replace with Nate-specific sensory detail — his phone screen, his own breathing, the sounds he's hyper-aware of as a podcaster. Keep 1596 (loon) only if it serves Nate's podcast-brain instinct to process sounds as content.

findings.evidence: 70 em-dash-containing prose lines / 130 total prose lines = 53.8%.
findings.problem: Ch 6 em-dash density is 53.8%. Over half of all prose lines contain an em-dash.
findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce em-dash lines from 70 to ~10. Use commas, colons, periods, or subordinate clauses instead.

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Preserve productive dialogue roughness; do not clean living speech into balanced thesis statements.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: The comedy is the creative risk and the creative achievement. These are men in their late forties who talk the way men in their late forties talk when there are no women present and the drinks have been flowing for hours: crude, raunchy, profane, sexually explicit, casually cruel, and occasionally accidentally beautiful. Do not clean this up. Do not soften the language. Do not make them witty where they would be vulgar. Do not make them eloquent where they would be clumsy. The profanity is not decoration — it is the texture of the friendship. When the second death breaks the comic register, the absence of that profane warmth should feel like a loss, not a relief. The murder, when it arrives, should be rendered with physical specificity — the water, the struggle, the sounds. The grief should be specific. The shame should be specific. The twenty-two-year-old guilt should be embodied, not summarized. If a scene makes the writer uncomfortable, that is a signal to stay in it longer, not to cut away.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Every euphemism for death, sex, bodily function, emotional state, or financial desperation must be replaced with the direct term or a character-specific avoidance that itself reveals something about the character. Cliff saying 'restructure' when he means 'I'm broke' is character work. The narrator describing Cliff as 'facing financial challenges' is sanitization.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the absolute norm. These are informal men in their late forties who have been friends for twenty-five years. Uncontracted forms should appear only for deliberate emphasis, Joel's occasional legal precision, or Mallick's public-persona overcorrection. If two consecutive lines of dialogue contain uncontracted forms and neither character has a stated reason for formality, at least one is wrong.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: No two consecutive chapters should have the same structural rhythm. After a dialogue-heavy chapter, lead the next with interiority or description. After an interview chapter, give the reader a memory or a quiet observation. After a comic set piece, let the next chapter breathe. The scene counts vary (2-3) and should feel varied — a two-scene chapter after a three-scene chapter creates natural pacing shift. The tonal break at Chapter 14 should feel like a gear change, not a gradual dimming.
prose_style_profile.diction: Muscular, specific, unpretentious. No literary posturing. The prose should sound like intelligent conversation, not workshopped fiction. Prefer concrete nouns over abstract ones. Prefer active constructions. Avoid decorative metaphor — when a metaphor appears, it should be load-bearing. The comedy demands precision: funny is specific, and vague is never funny.
forbidden_drift_patterns: Prestige-literary inflation — do not elevate the comic register into literary solemnity. A man flushing Viagra while a detective knocks on the door is funny. The prose should be funny. Do not describe it as if it were a scene from Dostoevsky.

Comedic exhaustion — do not let every paragraph contain a joke. Comedy needs rhythm, which means it needs non-comic beats to land against. Let some paragraphs be straightforwardly descriptive or emotional.
Interior monologue sprawl — close third is not stream of consciousness. The POV character's interiority should be selective and purposeful, not an open channel of every thought.
Simultaneous narration — do not describe characters 'simultaneously' feeling multiple things. Choose the dominant feeling and let the undertone be implicit.
Forensic procedure porn — this is not a police procedural. Keep investigation details specific but brief. The comedy and character are the vehicle, not the forensics.

Metaphor creep — do not let the same metaphor template appear more than twice in a chapter. If you've used a sports metaphor, a water metaphor, and a fire metaphor, check that none of them are doing the same structural work.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third person, rotating POV. Each chapter is locked to one character's interiority. No omniscient intrusions. The prose should take on the coloring of the POV character without becoming parody — Nate's chapters move faster and list more, Doug's chapters are quieter and more observational, Mallick's chapters shift between her two registers.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Conversational propulsion, not literary drift. The prose should move like someone telling you this story — not rushed, but never self-admiring. Vary sentence length aggressively: long compound sentences for the comic set pieces, short declarative hits for the turns. The humor lives in rhythm as much as content — the timing of a punchline is a prose-level decision.
sensory_bias: auditory — conversations, lake sounds, house creaks, the specific silence of a house where someone died
proprioceptive — hangovers, physical discomfort, adrenaline, the specific sensation of a body that hasn't slept
olfactory — booze, lake water, morning coffee, the basement must, the casserole
